interactions.summary: Jiang identifies monasteries as the opportunity that began the Viking Age: post-Roman wealth became concentrated again under Charlemagne, and monasteries were wealthy, trusted, landholding, divinely protected targets.
He adds the military-political opportunity: armored knights and feudal hierarchy made Europe vulnerable to river ships, and changing tactics would have meant changing the political order.

Jiang explains the end of the Viking Age as co-optation: successful raiders became aristocrats, intermarried with European nobility, converted to Christianity, and supplied military support to the powers they had once attacked.

speaker_notes.note: Segment is a brief non-substantive 'Okay?' transition and does not appear to be a separate student question.

claims.claim: After Rome, wealth was not simply gone; Jiang says it became distributed, then reconcentrated in monasteries as Charlemagne's kingdoms arose.
claims.temporal_scope: Post-Roman and Carolingian context in this lecture.

claims.claim: Monasteries were ideal Viking targets because they were wealthy banks, landowners, business centers, and religious treasuries protected more by sacred trust than by military force.
claims.temporal_scope: Explanation for Viking raids in this lecture.

claims.claim: Europe's feudal military hierarchy made adaptation hard because changing tactics against Vikings would have required changing the social hierarchy that armored knights supported.
The Viking Age ended when Vikings became successful enough to be absorbed into European nobility, Christianized, and turned into protectors of the same powers they had pressured.
claims.temporal_scope: Viking Age endpoint explanation in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: A monastery is not only a holy place; it is a bank, university, landlord, brewery, and treasure vault.
signature_moments.source_phrase: places of worship and of learning, but much more importantly, they are places where wealth is concentrated
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes Viking opportunism legible as institutional arbitrage rather than inexplicable savagery.

signature_moments.moment: Bandit wealth becomes legitimate only by turning into aristocracy.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Once you're an aristocrat, your wealth becomes legitimate
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It compresses Jiang's model of assimilation as the laundering of force into status.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang defines it as armored knights becoming landowning nobles who receive land grants and extract from farmers.
Religious spread process where elites convert first and then convert their followers and people.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang says the next class will explain why Viking culture influenced the British/English, French, Germans, and Russians; this lecture only previews the worldview.

uncertainty_notes.note: Transcript says farmers 'will not become slaves'; context suggests 'will now become' or become bound/dependent under debt, but the exact wording is damaged.
